quoted_status.full_text:
Amazon's internal metrics said customers waited under 60 seconds for customer service. Jeff Bezos picked up the phone in a meeting and waited more than 10 minutes.

The head of customer service had been defending the number. Bezos said "Ok, let's call." He dialed Amazon's 1-800 line on speaker. The room sat there for over ten minutes before a rep answered. The metric didn't survive the meeting.

Bezos has a saying: when the data and the anecdotes disagree, the anecdotes are usually right.

Metrics don't measure reality. They measure what you designed them to measure. Customer service dashboards commonly filter out abandoned calls, cap hold time at the IVR timeout, and start the clock after the menu tree completes. Every one of those choices pushes the average down. The customers hanging up at minute 9 are not in the denominator. The 60-second number was technically accurate and practically wrong.

That call broke through a defended metric in a way no spreadsheet could have. The head of CS had dashboards and a team whose job was to report that number going down quarter over quarter. Bezos had 10 minutes of hold music and a room full of people watching.

This is the executive test almost nobody runs. Call your own 1-800 line. Try to buy your own product in incognito. Every senior leader can do it in under 15 minutes. Almost none do, because the dashboards feel like the truth and the dashboards say things are fine.

The measurement got redesigned. Wait times actually fell.

When your data says you're winning and your customers say you're losing, the customers are right. The data was built by people whose job depends on it going down.

tweets.full_text:
We've seen this film before...

Nasdaq hit its 12th straight record close while companies with 0 AI expertise rebrand overnight and rip 200-600%

26 years ago, it was a similar script with ".com"

Then (1998-2000):
• April 9, 1998: K-Tel announced a pivot to online music sales and stock ripped +900%
• March 29, 1999: Computer Literacy rebranded to Fatbrain com and stock ripped +322%
• March 21, 2000 Goldman Sachs told clients to go 90% stocks
• April 17, 2000: Nasdaq had its biggest one-day point gain in history.

In 2026:
• April 2, 2026: Goldman Sachs told clients the worst of the selling is behind us
• April 15, 2026: Allbirds rebranded to NewBird AI to rent GPUs and stock ripped +582%
• April 16, 2026: Myseum rebranded to Myseum AI and stock ripped +200% pre-market
• April 16, 2026: Nasdaq closes at a new all-time high

The Nasdaq crashed 78% and bottomed 30 months after that April 17, 2000 rally...

tweets.full_text:
🚨 BREAKING: In a direct challenge to the United States, 16 Chinese scientific groups are calling for global COOPERATION in AI, and an open, fair, and inclusive global AI governance system:

According to the press release from the China Association for Science and Technology, 16 societies affiliated with it launched the 'Initiative of the Global Science and Technology Society on AI Governance.'

The initiative states that increasing the well-being of humanity should be the fundamental guiding principle for AI academic research and that all countries should participate equally in AI research and governance.

A quote that caught my attention:

"We strictly oppose technological hegemony, academic barriers, exclusive 'small circles,' and unreasonable technological monopolies."

This is clearly a nudge against the United States, which has recently taken a more aggressive position against China, limiting exports and academic partnerships in AI.

This initiative also reminded me of this week's podcast interview between @dwarkesh_sp and NVIDIA's Jensen Huang, which has gone viral (see a popular clip below), especially because Jensen appeared visibly bothered by Dwarkesh's adversarial positioning against China.

Jensen expressed a view similar to that proposed in this initiative: the only way to "win the AI race" is through a strong level of global interchange and collaboration.

As I've written before, the AI market has an inherent global character, and AI governance will only work if we reach a meaningful level of global consensus (in parallel to national AI efforts and strategies).

-

👉 To learn more about China's AI strategy and governance, join my newsletter's 93,800+ subscribers and my upcoming masterclass on China's AI policy and regulation (links below).

tweets.full_text:
I just saw ClickUp launch AI agents that work 24/7 without burning out.

They’re called Super Agents, and the idea feels simple and terrifying at the same time.

I describe the job. ClickUp builds the agent. I assign it tasks the same way I’d assign a junior hire. It gets to work. It sends emails. It schedules meetings. It updates tasks. It messages my team. It runs all day and all night without me touching anything.

What makes it genuinely useful (and not just another chatbot wrapper) is the memory architecture:

→ I get short-term memory for what’s happening right now in the conversation
→ I get working memory for the task it’s currently executing
→ I get long-term memory that persists what it’s learned about my team and workflows
→ I get episodic memory so it remembers past interactions and improves from my feedback

It also connects to 50+ apps and does enterprise search across them with permissions preserved. If I can’t see a file, the agent can’t see it either.

I also noticed the CEO of Codegen called it “the holy grail of what enterprises are chasing.”

I’m seeing reports that 10 million agents have already been created.

Link in comments.

tweets.full_text:
$AAOI just announced its Houston footprint is expanding to 900,000 sq ft with InP laser fab capacity growing ~350% by end of 2027.

InP lasers are the physical bottleneck of the entire AI optical supply chain. Global fab capacity sits in dozens of facilities. NVDA pre-secured all of $LITE’s EML output in 2024 and the rest of the hyperscalers are still scrambling for supply.

The new buildings will support up to 700,000 units per month of 800G and 1.6T transceivers. Laser fab, design, and assembly all under one roof.

(800G at $263, 1.6T at $344), 700k units per month pencils out to roughly $213M per month on a 50/50 mix, or closer to $240M on a 1.6T-heavy mix. A $2.5B to $2.9B annualized run rate from Houston alone.

Only fully vertical US producer in the space.

$AAOI $LITE $COHR $AXTI

tweets.full_text:
Simulink と Polyspace 専用 Copilot が昨日リリースされたわけですが、さっそく 5/12 に webinar があります！ぜひご覧ください。

登録はこちら👇から
生成AIで“設計から品質まで”を短縮！Simulink Copilot、Polyspace Copilot徹底紹介
https://t.co/dXvXJQ5smM

アジェンダ
R2026a で新たに登場した Simulink Copilot および Polyspace Copilot は、生成AIを活用することで、モデルベースデザインやソフトウェア検証の効率を大きく向上させる新しい支援製品です。

Simulink Copilot は、Simulink モデルを対象に、モデルの理解支援、エラー診断、設計に関するアドバイス、定型作業の自動化などを行い、これまで時間のかかっていたモデル理解や修正作業を強力にサポートします。

本セミナーの前半では、Simulink Copilot を中心に、基本機能のご紹介に加え、どのような開発シーンで役立つのか、従来の開発フローがどのように変わるのかといった観点から、具体的な活用例を交えて解説します。
後半では、Polyspace Copilot について紹介します。Visual Studio Code 上で解析結果を起点として、指摘内容を説明し、修正の提案から適用、正当化の作成までを支援する基本ワークフローを、デモを交えて紹介します。
